feedingProfile.preferredNpk: high-potassium liquid feed (e.g. 5-5-10) every fortnight for containers; in-ground trees rarely need feeding if mulched (apply nitrogen in early spring only when annual shoot growth is under ~20 cm / 8 in)

commonIssues: Plum is a deciduous tree; in-ground planting in a warm, sunny, sheltered spot is strongly preferred. For containers choose a compact/dwarfing variety; the recommendedMinLiters (90 L) reflects the RHS minimum pot of about 50 cm wide and 50 cm deep (~98 L for that volume) and is not a size for a standard or semi-dwarf tree.

Plums have little tolerance for wet, heavy or waterlogged soils and develop root-rotting diseases easily; water deeply but infrequently and never leave containers standing in water (overwaterRisk is high).

Most European plums (e.g. 'Victoria', 'Stanley', 'Mount Royal') are self-fertile, but fruit set and yield usually improve with a compatible European-plum pollinator flowering at the same time. European plums (Prunus domestica) and Japanese plums (Prunus salicina) generally do not cross-pollinate each other.
Blossoms open very early in spring and are easily killed by late frost, which can destroy an entire season's crop; choose a non-frost-pocket site and protect with fleece when frost is forecast.
European plums are relatively high-chill (roughly 700-1000 hours below ~7 C / 45 F), so they fruit poorly in mild-winter climates that do not meet their chilling requirement.
Container trees dry out far faster than in-ground trees and may need watering several times a week (even daily) in hot, dry weather despite the 7-day in-ground base interval.

source.citation: RHS — How to grow plums (grow your own): plums need a warm, sunny spot with fertile soil that does not waterlog; container plums (compact varieties such as 'Blue Tit') need a pot at least 50 cm wide and deep with loam-based compost; water containers regularly in dry spells April-August keeping compost evenly moist but never standing in water; feed containers with high-potassium liquid fertiliser every fortnight April-August (or general-purpose granular each spring); in-ground mulched trees generally need no feeding.

RHS — How to grow plums: minimum container is about 50 cm wide and 50 cm deep for compact varieties; a 50 cm-diameter, 50 cm-deep round pot is roughly 98 L, so recommendedMin/idealMin container volumes are set near that (90-125 L). Soil should be fertile, ideally slightly acidic pH 6-6.5, well-drained loam/clay that neither dries out nor waterlogs.

RHS — How to grow plums: blossom is vulnerable to frost in March-April (cover with fleece if frost forecast); avoid frost-prone sites. Many varieties ('Victoria', 'Czar', 'Warwickshire Drooper') are self-fertile. Harvest July-September when fruit is fully coloured and soft.

University of Minnesota Extension — Growing stone fruits in the home garden: European plums (Prunus domestica) are self-compatible and do not require a second variety, though cross-pollination can increase yields; stone-fruit trees need full sun for the most fruit; cold-hardy European cultivars (e.g. Mount Royal) are reliable to USDA zone 4; blossoms bloom very early and delicate flowers are easily frozen; space trees 12-20 ft apart; harvest mid-late August to early September.

University of Minnesota Extension — Growing stone fruits in the home garden: cold-hardy European plum cultivars are rated for USDA zone 4. Zone 4 average annual minimum temperature is about -34 to -29 C (-30 to -20 F); coldHardyMinC set conservatively to -29 C for typical European-plum hardiness. Blossoms are frost-sensitive.

University of Minnesota Extension — Growing stone fruits in the home garden: stone fruit trees need full sun to produce the most fruit (minimum half-day sun). targetSunHours 8 / minSunHours 6 reflect 'full sun' (6+ hours direct sun) for a fruit tree.

UC Statewide IPM Program — Cultural tips for growing plum and prune: young trees need up to 3-5 gallons of water per week; established trees are irrigated every 2-3 weeks (12-24 h to wet 3-6 ft deep), more often (every 3-5 days) in sandy soils and every ~1.5-2 weeks in heavier soils. European plums require about 700-1000 chilling hours (below 45 F / 7 C). Mature trees receive ~3-5 lb ammonium sulfate or ~2 lb urea per year. Trees need at least 150 frost-free days so spring blossoms are not damaged.

Confidence note: baseIntervalDays (7) is a conservative in-ground/large-container default reconciling RHS 'keep evenly moist, water regularly in dry spells' with UC IPM ranges (young trees ~weekly 3-5 gal; established trees every 2-3 weeks; sandy soils every 3-5 days). Actual interval varies strongly with soil type, container vs ground, rootstock and climate (containers often need watering several times a week or daily in heat).

Confidence note: heatStressC (35 C) is an estimate. The authoritative extension/RHS sources read for this profile do not state an explicit upper heat-stress temperature for Prunus domestica; the value is a conservative general fruit-tree heat-stress threshold and should be refined if a cited figure is found.
